# DeutschFlow — Lesson Review Rubric

**Version:** 1.0 · **Updated:** 2026-07-12 · **Status:** Active — score every lesson against this.
**Based on:** LESSON_BLUEPRINT · LESSON_CHECKLIST · EXERCISE_PATTERN_LIBRARY

> Every lesson is scored on the 12 dimensions below (1–5). **Target: average ≥ 4.0, no dimension below 3.** Gate: score before native review; re-score after. A lesson scoring avg < 3.0 returns to Draft. A lesson scoring 5.0 on all dimensions is Reference quality (use as a model for new authors).

---

## Scoring scale (universal)

| Score | Meaning | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Missing or seriously deficient — blocks learning | Return to Draft |
| **2** | Present but weak — confusing or inconsistent | Revise before review |
| **3** | Meets the standard — correct, clear, effective | Acceptable |
| **4** | Exceeds the standard — polished, thoughtful | Strong |
| **5** | Reference quality — the gold standard; use as an exemplar | Publish & model |

---

## Dimensions

### 1. Educational quality
*Does this lesson teach? Does someone who knows nothing leave with real ability?*
| 1 | Not teaching a capability — topic dump, grammar lecture, or word list |
| 2 | Capability is vague or unreachable in this lesson ("learn German nouns") |
| 3 | One clear, reachable capability; learner can produce it by the end |
| 4 | Capability is immediately actionable; exercises directly build it |
| 5 | Capability is memorable and real-world-evident; learner finishes feeling "I could do this right now in Germany" |

### 2. Communication focus
*Is the capability about **doing** something in German, not **knowing about** German?*
| 1 | Teaches grammar/vocab as an end; no communicative outcome |
| 2 | Labels a "speaking goal" but exercises are all comprehension-only |
| 3 | One real communicative outcome; exercises include at least one production item |
| 4 | Dialogue models the outcome; speaking stage gives a genuine conversational turn |
| 5 | Every stage — from dialogue through celebration — serves the communicative goal; the lesson reads like a scaffolded real interaction |

### 3. Vocabulary quality
*Are the words right, right-sized, and taught well?*
| 1 | No vocabulary section or a dump of 15+ unrelated items |
| 2 | Over/under the 8–12 count; missing articles or no "watch out" notes |
| 3 | 8–12 words, chunked ≤6, each with article + example + common-confusion note |
| 4 | Words are clearly extracted from the dialogue; concrete/abstract ordering; cognates leveraged |
| 5 | Every word is high-frequency, level-appropriate, and essential to the capability; the "watch out" notes anticipate proven beginner errors |

### 4. Grammar sequencing
*Is grammar discovered, minimal, and in the right place?*
| 1 | Grammar-first lecturing — rule before exposure; or 2+ patterns in one lesson |
| 2 | Pattern present but not discovered (no call-back to dialogue); terminology overload |
| 3 | One pattern, discovered (dialogue lines → table), one why-line; no jargon dump |
| 4 | Pattern flows naturally from the dialogue; the table is minimal; Emma expands only on request |
| 5 | The learner genuinely feels "I noticed it myself"; discovery is elegant, the explanation is one elegant why-line |

### 5. Dialogue naturalness
*Could this conversation happen in real life?*
| 1 | No dialogue (or a single example sentence) |
| 2 | Dialogue exists but is stilted, unnatural, or reads like a textbook exchange |
| 3 | 6–10 natural lines; realistic context; uses only known + this-lesson vocabulary |
| 4 | Dialogue feels like it could occur in real life at normal speed; 2–3 distinct speakers |
| 5 | The dialogue is the lesson's heart — authentic, memorable, and the learner feels they witnessed a real interaction; every downstream piece traces back to it |

### 6. Exercise quality
*Do exercises teach, or just drill? Are they the right types in the right order?*
| 1 | No exercises or one repeat-the-word drill |
| 2 | Wrong mix for the level; missing core types (MCQ/Matching/Ordering/Fill-in at A1) |
| 3 | 4 core types in the right order; each has purpose + expected mistake + feedback + Emma hint |
| 4 | Distractors are educational; difficulty rises; feedback is specific and teachable |
| 5 | Exercises feel like part of the lesson story — each one a stepping stone toward the capability, never busywork; distractor design is masterful |

### 7. Speaking quality
*Is the speaking stage welcoming, optional, and pedagogically sound?*
| 1 | No speaking stage or forced/blocking speaking |
| 2 | Speaking present but no skip path; or Emma script is one-size-fits-all |
| 3 | Speaking optional with visible skip; Emma branches correct/incomplete/stuck/skip |
| 4 | Emma models the target, praises the attempt, and invites a retry; suggestion chips scaffold |
| 5 | The speaking stage feels like a genuine mini-conversation; the learner finishes it feeling braver than when they started (SPEAKING_GUIDELINES confidence ladder) |

### 8. Emma quality
*Is Emma a tutor, not an answer machine? Is her voice consistent?*
| 1 | Emma absent or out-of-voice (lecturing, revealing answers immediately, guilt-tone) |
| 2 | Emma present but erratic — sometimes a tutor, sometimes an answer key |
| 3 | Emma follows the hint ladder; corrects one thing with a reason; voice matches `emma-script-template.md` |
| 4 | Emma appears only where needed (≤6 touches); each touchpoint is a genuine teaching moment |
| 5 | Emma reads like a real kind tutor — the right level of warmth, the right hint at the right time; the learner feels supported, never evaluated |

### 9. Microcopy quality
*Does the text itself teach and encourage?*
| 1 | No microcopy, raw short strings, or institutional tone |
| 2 | Present but inconsistent — mixed voices, some guilt/sarcasm/spammy tone |
| 3 | Consistent DeutschFlow voice: friendly, calm, intelligent, encouraging; sentence case; active |
| 4 | Copy is tight — no filler lines; stage transitions feel smooth; the tone scales with the learner's progression |
| 5 | The microcopy is itself a teaching tool — warm without being cloying, brief without being abrupt; the learner feels spoken to, not processed |

### 10. Accessibility
*Can every learner complete this lesson?*
| 1 | Fails basic accessibility (colour-only status, no labels, no keyboard path) |
| 2 | Some elements labelled but inconsistent; audio-dependent with no text fallback |
| 3 | Keyboard-operable; all status uses icon+text; captions for audio; 44px targets; focus-visible |
| 4 | Live regions where feedback occurs; reduced-motion-friendly; SR flow tested |
| 5 | Accessibility is structural, not retrofitted — every exercise, stage, and Emma interaction is usable by keyboard/SR/touch with no degradation |

### 11. Consistency (with the rest of DeutschFlow)
*Would this lesson feel like it belongs in the same product as all the others?*
| 1 | Different format, terminology, voice, stage order, or style |
| 2 | Follows the template but deviates in multiple places without documented reason |
| 3 | Faithfully follows `lesson-template.md`, `exercise-pattern-library`, and `emma-script-template` |
| 4 | Consistent in every section; vocabulary/grammar terminology matches the rest of the curriculum |
| 5 | A model of consistency — another author could use this lesson as their template and produce near-identical-format lessons |

### 12. CEFR alignment
*Is the content level-appropriate per CEFR can-do descriptors?*
| 1 | Misaligned (B2 grammar in A1, trivial exercise set for C1) |
| 2 | Level-tagged but content drifts (too hard/too easy in multiple places) |
| 3 | Level-appropriate dialogue length, vocabulary, grammar scope, instruction language, exercise difficulty |
| 4 | The lesson feels precisely targeted to its level — pacing and scaffolding match CEFR expectations |
| 5 | The lesson would satisfy a CEFR examiner that this capability is genuinely at the stated level |

---

| Dimension | Score (1–5) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Educational quality | | |

| 5. Dialogue naturalness | | |

| 12. CEFR alignment | | |
| **Average** | **∑ / 12** | |

**Reviewer:** ____________ **Date:** ____________

**Decision:** [ ] Publish (≥4.0 avg, no dim <3)  [ ] Minor revisions (≥3.5 avg)  [ ] Return to Draft (<3.5 avg)

---

## How to use this rubric (operational)
1. Author completes the lesson + runs `lesson-checklist.md`.
2. Reviewer (second pair of eyes — preferably a CEFR-trained instructor or curriculum lead) scores the 12 dimensions. Any score of 1 or 2 must include a specific remediation note.
3. Average ≥4.0 + no dimension <3 → **Author-Ready** (passes to native review).
4. Native review passed + audio confirmed → **Production-Ready**.
5. Scores of 5 are rare — they mean "use this lesson as the example for training new authors."
6. Re-score after any significant revision or at curriculum milestone reviews (every 10 lessons).

## Reference: scores for the gold-standard lesson
*Lesson 1 target (self-review):* avg 4.2–4.5 (docked slightly on accessibility — needs engine-side live regions, and on audio — depends on audio assets). This sets the bar: a 4.0 lesson **feels as good** as the reference; a 5.0 lesson **exceeds** it on every dimension.
